Therefore, the set of candidates for achieving $K_{max}$ consists only of the two sequences starting at 1 and 2 (if they exist within $1$ to $N-2$).

#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <cmath>
#include <limits>

using namespace std;

// Helper function to calculate the cost for a specific starting index (offset)
long long calculate_cost(const vector<long long>& nums, int start_offset) {
    long long total_cost = 0;
    int n = nums.size();
    
    // Iterate over indices i = start_offset, start_offset + 2, ... up to N-2
    for (int i = start_offset; i <= n - 2; i += 2) {
        // A peak at i requires nums[i] > nums[i-1] and nums[i] > nums[i+1]
        
        // Determine the required minimum value R for nums[i]
        // R must be at least max(nums[i-1], nums[i+1]) + 1
        long long required_base = max(nums[i-1], nums[i+1]);
        long long required_value = required_base + 1;
        
        // Calculate operations needed
        if (nums[i] < required_value) {
            long long operations = required_value - nums[i];
            total_cost += operations;
        }
    }
    return total_cost;
}

long long solve(std::vector<long long> nums) {
    int n = nums.size();
    if (n < 3) {
        return 0; // No possible special indices
    }

    // Potential peak indices are {1, 2, ..., N-2}. Length L = N-2.
    int L = n - 2;

    // K_max = ceil(L / 2.0)
    // In integer math, K_max = (L + 1) / 2
    int K_max = (L + 1) / 2;

    // Calculate cost for sequence starting at 1 (Odd indices: 1, 3, 5, ...)
    long long cost1 = calculate_cost(nums, 1);
    
    // Calculate cost for sequence starting at 2 (Even indices: 2, 4, 6, ...)
    long long cost2 = calculate_cost(nums, 2);
    
    // Check sizes of the sets I1 and I2
    // Size of I1 (starting at 1): (L + 1) / 2
    int size1 = (L + 1) / 2;
    
    // Size of I2 (starting at 2): L / 2
    int size2 = L / 2;

    if (size1 == K_max && size2 == K_max) {
        // Both achieve maximum density. Choose minimum cost.
        return min(cost1, cost2);
    } else if (size1 == K_max) {
        // Only I1 achieves K_max.
        return cost1;
    } else { 
        // Since K_max = max(size1, size2), if size1 != K_max, size2 must be K_max (only possible if L=0, but L>=1).
        // If L >= 1, K_max = size1. 
        // If L=1, K_max=1. size1=1, size2=0. Returns cost1.
        // If L=2, K_max=1. size1=1, size2=1. Returns min(cost1, cost2).
        // If L=3, K_max=2. size1=2, size2=1. Returns cost1.
        
        // This branch should theoretically only be hit if K_max was calculated incorrectly or L=0, 
        // but defensively return cost2 if size2 matches K_max.
        return cost2;
    }
}

If $L$ is even, $L=2k$. $K_{max}=k$. $|I_1|=k, |I_2|=k$. We compare both.

This covers all cases based on parity of $L=N-2$.

If $L$ is odd: Return `cost1`.
If $L$ is even: Return `min(cost1, cost2)`.
